title: Apple and Samsung benefit as memory shortage pushes smartphone shipments to historic lows
gist: Global smartphone shipments fell 11% year-over-year in Q2 2026 to their lowest level since 2013 as surging DRAM/NAND prices - driven by manufacturers diverting memory supply to the AI compute boom - squeeze budget phones hardest, while Apple and Samsung held share by protecting flagship pricing.

insights.claim: Counterpoint reports global smartphone shipments fell 11% year-over-year in Q2 2026, the weakest second quarter since 2013; Omdia separately reports a smaller 4% decline for the same period, with both firms agreeing on the direction though not the magnitude.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: A double-digit shipment decline in a mature market signals a genuine supply-driven demand shock rather than normal seasonal softness, relevant to component suppliers and device OEM earnings.
insights.locator: According to Counterpoint, this substantial drop

insights.claim: Analysts attribute the smartphone shipment decline to rising DRAM and NAND chip prices, as manufacturers have shifted memory supply toward the AI computing boom, leaving fewer components available for consumer devices like smartphones and PCs.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: A direct read-through from AI data-center capex to consumer electronics margins and volumes: memory suppliers benefit while device OEMs and consumers absorb higher costs.
insights.locator: Analysts place the blame for this drop squarely

insights.claim: Omdia reports that memory now accounts for up to half of total manufacturing cost on phones priced at $500 or less, versus just over a quarter of cost on flagship devices, meaning budget phones have seen faster and larger price increases than flagship models.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: The budget smartphone segment is disproportionately exposed to the memory cost shock, likely accelerating exits or price hikes among lower-margin OEMs (Oppo, Vivo, Xiaomi) relative to flagship-focused players.
insights.locator: A recent report from market research firm Omdia

insights.claim: In Q2 2026, per Counterpoint, Samsung reclaimed the #1 global smartphone position with 24% share and Apple's shipments grew 3%, while Oppo, Vivo, and Xiaomi all declined; Omdia separately puts Samsung at 22% share and Apple at 20%. Google's Pixel shipments, though outside the top five, grew 16% year-over-year on strong Pixel 10 sales.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Scale and flagship pricing power are becoming decisive competitive advantages during the memory shortage; smaller OEMs without pricing power are losing volume faster.

insights.claim: Apple held smartphone shipment growth by keeping prices stable for its current iPhone generation while most other OEMs raised prices, a pricing-power advantage the article notes may not hold once Apple releases new iPhones in the coming months.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Apple's ability to keep prices flat this cycle is a temporary buffer, not a permanent shield from memory cost inflation - watch for price action on the next iPhone launch as a signal of how much cost pressure Apple ultimately passes to consumers.
insights.locator: Apple saw shipments grow by 3 percent last quarter

insights.claim: Samsung sustained shipment volume by focusing on flagship phones - the Galaxy S26 series, particularly the Ultra variant, outsold last year's models despite higher prices - while also using aggressive promotions on budget devices in markets like India and the Middle East, even as it raised prices on those same budget tiers.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Samsung's dual strategy (flagship premiumization plus targeted regional promotion) is a template for how large OEMs can offset shortage-driven cost inflation without losing volume.
insights.locator: Samsung's shipments were up as well

themes.theme: Structural shift toward longer replacement cycles

insights.claim: The author argues that even if AI investment turns out to be a bubble, smartphone makers will likely spend the next year eliminating low-margin budget phones and raising prices on remaining models, pushing the industry toward treating smartphones 'more like appliances' bought less often and supported longer - reinforced by Samsung and Google now matching Apple's seven-year software support window.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: A structural lengthening of smartphone replacement cycles would compress long-run unit volume growth for the industry, favoring OEMs with services/ecosystem revenue over pure hardware volume plays.
insights.quote: Increasingly, it looks like AI hysteria is going to be a watershed moment for smartphone makers.
insights.locator: Increasingly, it looks like AI hysteria

insights.claim: Every analysis cited in the article, including Counterpoint's, expects the memory component shortage driving smartphone price increases to continue at least into next year.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: A multi-quarter (not one-off) memory shortage supports continued pricing power for DRAM/NAND suppliers and continued margin pressure for phone and PC OEMs into 2027.
